{{< pagebreak >}}

# Day 5 — Make the easier choice more visible {#day5}

```{=typst}
#wcg-lead[Today takes pressure off your willpower and puts it on your surroundings.]
#wcg-lesson-art("environment", caption: [A phone set aside; a short list made easier to reach])
```

## Why this matters

The choice that happens automatically — the "default" — can strongly shape what
people end up doing.^10^ And because habits are triggered by cues,^3^ changing the
cues and the ease of a choice can quietly change the choice itself.

So you do not have to be more disciplined. You can work in both directions: add a
little friction to an automatic purchase, and remove a little friction from the
choice you would rather make.

## An everyday example

Logging out of stored payment details adds a few seconds to a purchase — just
enough to invite a pause. Moving a shopping app off your home screen means you no
longer tap it by reflex. Small changes to your surroundings do the work that
willpower used to.

```{=typst}
#wcg-action[
  Pick one automatic purchase and make it a little harder. Then pick one calmer
  choice and make it a little easier. Use the checklist for ideas.
]
```

> **Notice.**
>
> What makes this purchase so easy for me right now?

You are arranging your surroundings so the calmer choice needs less effort. That
is smart design, not self-control.

{{< include assets/worksheets/ws-5-environment-friction.qmd >}}
